teaching.title: Strings Deep Dive
teaching.analogy: A string is like a necklace of beads — each bead is one character. You can count beads, pick specific ones by position, or rearrange them.
teaching.content: Strings are sequences of characters. Each character has an index starting from 0. You can access individual characters, find the length, and slice parts out.

examples.title: String Indexing
examples.explanation: Each character has a position starting from 0.
examples.code:
word = 'Python'
print(word[0])    # P
print(word[3])    # h
print(len(word))  # 6

teaching.funFact: Strings are IMMUTABLE in Python — you can't change a character in place. You must create a new string! 🔒

teaching.title: String Methods
teaching.analogy: String methods are like built-in spell effects. .upper() shouts, .lower() whispers, .replace() swaps words, .split() breaks text into a list.
teaching.content: Python strings have dozens of built-in methods: .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), .replace(old, new), .split(), .find(), .count(), .startswith(), .endswith().

examples.title: Common Methods
examples.explanation: Methods don't change the original — they return a NEW string.
examples.code:
name = 'Hello World'
print(name.upper())          # HELLO WORLD
print(name.lower())          # hello world
print(name.replace('H','J')) # Jello World
print(name.split(' '))       # ['Hello', 'World']

teaching.funFact: .strip() removes whitespace from both ends. Super useful for cleaning user input! 🧹

teaching.title: String Slicing
teaching.analogy: Slicing is like cutting a piece of ribbon. You pick the start position, the end position, and snip! The original stays untouched.
teaching.content: Slicing syntax: string[start:end]. start is included, end is excluded. Omit start = from beginning. Omit end = to the end.

examples.title: Slicing Examples
examples.code:
s = 'ABCDEF'
print(s[1:4])  # BCD
print(s[:3])   # ABC
print(s[3:])   # DEF
print(s[::-1]) # FEDCBA (reverse!)

teaching.funFact: s[::-1] reverses any string! This is a classic Python interview question. 🔄

mcq.q: What is 'Hello'[1]?
mcq.explanation: Index 0='H', 1='e'. String indexing starts at 0.

mcq.q: What does .upper() do?
mcq.explanation: .upper() converts every character to uppercase.

mcq.q: What is 'Python'[1:4]?
mcq.explanation: Index 1='y', 2='t', 3='h'. End (4) is excluded. Result: 'yth'.

mcq.q: Are strings mutable (changeable) in Python?
mcq.explanation: Strings are immutable. You can't change individual characters — only create new strings.

mcq.q: What does 'hello world'.split(' ') return?
mcq.explanation: .split(' ') breaks the string at spaces into a list of words.

coding.title: Reverse It
coding.objective: How do you reverse a string s? Type the slice notation.
coding.hint: Step -1 means go backwards from end to start.

options: Strings are immutable — can't change characters. Use: name = 'B' + name[1:]
debugging.answer: Strings are immutable — can't change characters. Use: name = 'B' + name[1:]
debugging.hint: Strings can't be modified in place. Create a new string instead.
Python is case sensitive. String methods are all lowercase.

prediction.explanation: .upper() returns a NEW string. The original x stays 'hello'. y gets 'HELLO'.

teaching.title: What is a Function?
teaching.analogy: A function is like a recipe card. You write it once, then cook (call) it whenever you want. The recipe can take ingredients (parameters) and serve a dish (return value).
teaching.content: Functions are reusable blocks of code. Define with 'def', give it a name and optional parameters, then call it by name. Functions keep code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself).

examples.title: Define & Call
examples.explanation: def creates it. name() calls it.
examples.code:
def greet():
    print('Hello!')

greet()   # Hello!
greet()   # Hello!  (reusable!)

teaching.funFact: The average software project has thousands of functions. Netflix has over 700 microservices, each packed with functions! 📺

teaching.title: Parameters & Arguments
teaching.analogy: Parameters are empty slots on the recipe card. Arguments are the actual ingredients you fill in when cooking. The recipe says 'add FLOUR'. You add '2 cups of wheat flour'.
teaching.content: Parameters are variables listed in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values you pass when calling. They let functions work with different data each time.

examples.title: Parameters in Action
examples.explanation: name is a parameter. 'Alex' is an argument.
examples.code:
def greet(name):
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')

greet('Alex')    # Hello, Alex!
greet('Neo')     # Hello, Neo!

teaching.funFact: Python supports default parameters: def greet(name='World'). If you call greet(), it uses 'World' automatically! 😎

teaching.title: Return Values
teaching.analogy: return is like the waiter bringing your dish BACK to you. Without return, the function does its work but gives you nothing back — the result stays inside.
teaching.content: return sends a value BACK to where the function was called. Without return, the function returns None. The function STOPS running at the return line.

examples.title: Return vs Print
examples.explanation: return gives back a value you can store. print just displays.
examples.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)  # result = 8
print(result)       # 8

teaching.funFact: A function can return multiple values! return x, y returns a tuple. Python is one of few languages that allows this. 🎁

mcq.q: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
mcq.explanation: 'def' is the keyword to define functions in Python.

mcq.q: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
options: Parameter is in def, argument is when calling
mcq.explanation: Parameter: variable in the definition. Argument: actual value passed when calling.

mcq.q: What does a function return if there's no return statement?
mcq.explanation: Without an explicit return, Python functions return None by default.

mcq.q: Can a function call another function?
mcq.explanation: Functions can absolutely call other functions — this is called function composition!

mcq.q: What happens after a return statement executes?
mcq.explanation: return immediately exits the function. No code after it runs.

coding.title: Function Keyword
coding.objective: What keyword starts a function definition? Type it.

coding.title: Return Value
coding.objective: def double(x): return x * 2. What does double(7) return?
coding.starterCode:
def double(x):
    return x * 2

print(double(7))
# Output: ___

coding.title: No Return
coding.objective: def say(): print('Hi'). What does result = say() store?
coding.starterCode:
def say():
    print('Hi')

result = say()
print(result)
# Output: ___
coding.hint: No return statement = function returns None.

debugging.brokenCode:
def add(a, b)
    return a + b
options: Missing colon after parameters: def add(a, b):
debugging.answer: Missing colon after parameters: def add(a, b):
debugging.hint: Like if and for, function definitions need a colon at the end.

options: Missing parentheses: greet() — need () to CALL a function
debugging.answer: Missing parentheses: greet() — need () to CALL a function
debugging.hint: 'greet' alone is a reference. 'greet()' calls/executes it.

prediction.code:
def square(n):
    return n * n

x = square(4)
y = square(3)
print(x + y)

prediction.explanation: 3>7 is False, so first return skipped. return b → 7. This is a max() function!

teaching.title: Variable Scope — Local vs Global
teaching.analogy: Scope is like rooms in a house. A variable created in the kitchen (inside a function) stays IN the kitchen. You can't use it in the living room (outside the function). But a variable in the hallway (global) can be seen from any room.
teaching.content: Local variables exist only inside the function where they're created. Global variables are defined outside all functions and can be read anywhere. Modifying a global inside a function requires the 'global' keyword.

examples.title: Local vs Global
examples.explanation: Local variables can't be accessed outside their function.
examples.code:
x = 10  # global

def my_func():
    y = 5   # local — only exists inside here
    print(x) # can READ global ✅
    print(y) # local works ✅

my_func()
print(x)  # ✅ global
print(y)  # ❌ NameError! y doesn't exist here

examples.title: Modifying Global
examples.explanation: Use 'global' keyword to modify a global variable inside a function.
examples.code:
score = 0  # global

def add_point():
    global score
    score = score + 1  # modifies the global

add_point()
add_point()
print(score)  # 2

teaching.funFact: Overusing 'global' is considered bad practice — it makes code hard to debug. Prefer passing values as arguments and returning results instead! 🏆

teaching.title: Default Parameters
teaching.analogy: Default parameters are like a coffee machine's default settings. If you don't specify anything, it makes medium strength coffee. You can override it anytime you want something different.
teaching.content: Default parameter values are set in the function definition with =. If the caller doesn't provide an argument for that parameter, the default is used. Default params must come AFTER required params.

examples.title: Default in Action
examples.explanation: Calling with and without the optional argument.
examples.code:
def greet(name, greeting='Hello'):
    print(f'{greeting}, {name}!')

greet('Alex')           # Hello, Alex!
greet('Neo', 'Hi')      # Hi, Neo!
greet('Sam', 'Welcome') # Welcome, Sam!

examples.title: Multiple Defaults
examples.explanation: All defaults must come after required parameters.
examples.code:
def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(5))    # 25 (5²)
print(power(3, 3)) # 27 (3³)

teaching.funFact: Python's print() uses default params: print(value, sep=' ', end='\n'). That's why print('a', end='') doesn't add a newline — you're overriding the default! 🤯

teaching.title: *args — Variable Arguments
teaching.analogy: *args is like a bag that holds any number of items. You don't know in advance how many things someone will hand you — so instead of specific slots, you carry a bag that expands as needed.
teaching.content: *args lets a function accept any number of positional arguments. Inside the function, args is a tuple of all the values passed. The * is the magic — args is just a naming convention.

examples.title: *args in Action
examples.explanation: Call with any number of arguments.
examples.code:
def add_all(*args):
    total = 0
    for n in args:
        total += n
    return total

print(add_all(1, 2))        # 3
print(add_all(1, 2, 3, 4))  # 10
print(add_all(5))            # 5

teaching.funFact: Python's built-in print() uses *args internally — that's how print('a', 'b', 'c') works with any number of values! 🐍

mcq.q: A variable created inside a function is called ___.
mcq.explanation: Scope Intel: Variables created inside a function are 'local' — they only exist while that function runs and are destroyed after. They can't be accessed from outside the function.

mcq.q: What keyword is needed to MODIFY a global variable inside a function?
mcq.explanation: Global Keyword: Without 'global', assigning to a variable inside a function creates a new LOCAL variable. Use 'global varname' to tell Python you want to modify the actual global.

mcq.q: def greet(name, greeting='Hi'): ... What happens if you call greet('Alex')?
mcq.explanation: Default Parameter: If a caller doesn't provide greeting, Python uses the default 'Hi'. You only need to pass it if you want something different. Defaults make functions flexible!

mcq.q: In def f(a, b=10), which is the required parameter?
mcq.explanation: Parameter Order: 'a' has no default so it's required. 'b' has a default of 10 so it's optional. Required params always come BEFORE default params.

mcq.q: What type is *args inside a function?
mcq.explanation: *args Intel: Inside the function, *args is a TUPLE containing all the extra positional arguments passed. Tuples are immutable — you can iterate over args but can't modify it.

coding.title: Spot the Scope
coding.objective: Is a variable created inside a function accessible outside? Type: yes or no.
coding.starterCode:
def f():
    secret = 42
f()
print(secret)  # works? ___
coding.hint: secret is a local variable — it only exists while f() is running.

coding.title: Default Parameter
coding.objective: def power(base, exp=2): return base**exp. What does power(4) return?
coding.starterCode:
def power(base, exp=2):
    return base ** exp

print(power(4))
# Output: ___

coding.title: *args Count
coding.objective: def count(*args): return len(args). What does count(1,2,3,4,5) return?
coding.hint: 5 arguments were passed, so args is (1,2,3,4,5) — length 5.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug:

count = 0
def increment():
    count = count + 1

increment()
print(count)
options: Need 'global count' inside the function to modify the global variable
debugging.answer: Need 'global count' inside the function to modify the global variable
debugging.hint: Without 'global count', Python treats count inside the function as a new local variable — causing an UnboundLocalError.

options: Default parameters must come AFTER required ones: def greet(name, greeting='Hello')
debugging.answer: Default parameters must come AFTER required ones: def greet(name, greeting='Hello')
debugging.hint: In Python, required params (no defaults) must always come BEFORE optional params (with defaults).

prediction.code:
x = 'global'
def f():
    x = 'local'
    print(x)

f()
print(x)
prediction.explanation: Inside f(), x = 'local' creates a NEW local x — doesn't change the global. f() prints 'local', then outside print(x) shows the unchanged global 'global'.
First call passes 2 args → len=2. Second passes 5 args → len=5. *args captures all of them.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE ARCHIVIST
teaching.analogy: The Archivist guards the Spell Library. Every string method and function recipe is in its vault. Prove you've mastered them all!
teaching.content: This boss tests string indexing, slicing, methods, function definitions, parameters, return values, and the interplay between them.

examples.title: Your mastery
examples.code:
✅ String indexing & slicing
✅ .upper(), .lower(), .split(), .replace()
✅ def, parameters, arguments
✅ return values vs print
✅ Function composition

teaching.funFact: Alan Turing proved that any computable function can be built from simpler functions. Your functions can build anything! 🧠

mcq.q: What does 'Hello'[1:3] return?
mcq.explanation: Index 1='e', 2='l'. End (3) excluded. Result: 'el'.

mcq.q:
def f(x): return x**2
print(f(5) + f(3))
What does 'a,b,c'.split(',') return?
mcq.explanation: Split at commas → ['a', 'b', 'c'].

mcq.q:
def add(a,b): return a+b
add(2,3)
print(add(2,3))
mcq.explanation: First call returns 5 but it's not stored/printed. Second call prints 5.

mcq.q: Can you use a function's return value as a string method?
mcq.explanation: If a function returns a string, you can chain methods: get_name().upper().

coding.title: Boss: Combo
coding.objective: def shout(text): return text.upper(). What does shout('hello') return?

debugging.description:
🚨 Boss Bug:

def greet(name):
    return 'Hello ' + name

print(greet())
options: greet() is missing the required 'name' argument
debugging.answer: greet() is missing the required 'name' argument
debugging.hint: greet expects 1 argument (name) but was called with 0.
